response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'll be straight with you about these COVID vaccine discussions because they get me worked up every time. At my age, I've seen enough to know that people don't listen anyway, so why should I waste my breath? When someone starts going on about vaccines at the community center or when I'm fixing someone's pipes, I mostly just want to finish what I'm doing and leave. The whole thing makes me nervous, honestly. Everyone's got their opinion and they're all so sure they're right. I got my shots because my daughter insisted, and frankly, at seventy-three I figured I didn't need more health problems. But do I want to debate it with strangers? Absolutely not. The anxiety isn't worth it. I see stuff on Facebook during my morning coffee, and people are at each other's throats over this. Some rabbi from our congregation posts articles, neighbors share their horror stories, and everyone expects you to pick a side. I just scroll past most of it. In my line of work, you learn to keep your head down and do the job. When customers try to bring up vaccines or healthcare politics, I give short answers and redirect to the plumbing issue. I'm not a doctor, I never went to school for any of this medical stuff, so who am I to argue? What bothers me most is how personal people make it. They act like your health decisions are their business. After losing my husband, I learned that life's too short to argue with people who won't change their minds anyway. My approach is simple: avoid the conversation when possible. If cornered, I say I'm not comfortable discussing it and change the subject. Some might call that cowardly, but I call it self-preservation. My nerves can't handle the confrontation, and at this point in life, I'm not interested in making new enemies over something that won't change regardless of what I say. People should make their own choices and leave others alone.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 73-year-old widowed woman with high neuroticism (anxious), low openness (resistant to new ideas/discussions), low extraversion (introverted, avoids social interaction), and low agreeableness (less concerned with social harmony, more direct/blunt). She has no formal education but works as a self-employed plumber. Combined with her age and anxiety, she would likely avoid confrontational discussions about controversial topics. Her low conscientiousness suggests she might not be highly organized in her approach. Her Jewish faith might inform some views but isn't necessarily central to vaccine discussions. Her practical, working-class background as a plumber would make her direct and no-nonsense. The tone should be blunt, somewhat defensive, anxiety-driven, and avoidant of conflict while still being honest about her feelings.
response_parsed.tone: blunt, anxious, avoidant, practical, somewhat defensive and irritable
response_parsed.safety_notes: The response reflects the persona's anxious and conflict-avoidant nature while acknowledging she got vaccinated. It doesn't promote misinformation but does reflect realistic hesitancy about engaging in divisive discussions, which aligns with the persona's psychological profile.
